title: Precision Component Machining via Engine Lathe
overview: A hands-on manufacturing project focused on transforming raw cylindrical mild steel stock into a precise, multi-diameter machine component using manual subtractive manufacturing techniques.
problem_statement: Addressing the fundamental challenge of manually converting raw metal stock into a functional, dimensionally accurate part without relying on automated CNC machinery to meet modern mechanical assembly standards.
objectives: Master operational controls and safety protocols of an industrial Engine Lathe.
Execute precision material removal to achieve target stepped diameters.
Maintain strict dimensional accuracy and optimal surface finish.
engineering_process: Setup of workpiece in 3-jaw chuck, calibration of spindle speed (315 RPM), execution of sequential longitudinal passes with coolant, and continuous verification using Vernier calipers.
technical_details: Mild Steel Cylindrical Rod; Target diameters: 20.18 mm and 23.18 mm; Cut Depths: 0.4mm–0.5mm.
design_decisions: Utilized sequential shallow cuts instead of aggressive deep cuts to prevent workpiece deflection and tool wear.
challenges: Managing potential tool deflection on the 80mm overhang by adjusting feed rates.
results: Successfully manufactured the component within manual machining tolerances.
key_learning: Gained an understanding of mechanical feedback (vibration, chip formation) and the principles of Design-for-Manufacturability.
